required.title: [For Student]
required.author: Miguel Grinberg or equivalent web framework tutorial

required.title: [For Student] Git (git-scm.com) and a GitHub or GitLab account
[For Student] An AI coding assistant (GitHub Copilot, Claude, or equivalent)

suggested.title: [For Student] An API to build with

narration_prompts.detail: What problem does it solve? Who uses it? Walk through the architecture — how do the pieces fit together? Show the Git commit history. What was the hardest technical challenge and how did you solve it?
Show your Git log. Explain your branching strategy. Show a commit where you fixed a bug — what was the bug, how did you find it, how did the fix work?

narration_prompts.prompt: Show an API integration you built.
narration_prompts.detail: What API did you use? What data does your program access? How does it process and present the data?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe your use of AI coding tools.
narration_prompts.detail: Where did AI accelerate your work? Where did it produce errors you had to fix? How do you decide when to use AI assistance and when to write code yourself?

narration_prompts.prompt: Report on teaching someone to build.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you teach? How did it go? What did the learner struggle with? How did you adapt your explanation? What did they produce?

cross_connections.description: Clean code, version control discipline, finished projects — craftsmanship applied to the digital medium.

The ability to build digital products is the operational foundation of modern entrepreneurship. The student with a shipped project has demonstrated the core entrepreneurial capability: turning an idea into a working reality.

cross_connections.target_name: AI as an Economic and Political Force
cross_connections.description: AI-assisted building practiced here is the practical application of the AI understanding from 4.2.5.
The portfolio of completed work demonstrates the builder orientation that is a core component of digital sovereignty.

content.why_this_matters:
You can write Python programs, analyze data in spreadsheets, build web pages, and automate tasks. You are a digital builder. Now you cross the final gap: from exercises and small projects to building real systems that solve real problems for real users.

A real project is different from an exercise. It has users (even if the user is just you). It must work reliably. It must be maintained over time. It requires design decisions, trade-offs, and the discipline to ship something imperfect rather than endlessly polish something unreleased. The gap between "I could build this" and "I built this" is the gap between intention and execution. Standing Ground closes it.

You are also going to learn professional tools — version control with Git, working with APIs, database fundamentals — and you are going to use AI tools as building accelerators while maintaining your own understanding of what you have built. The capstone: you teach someone else to build, because the test of mastery is transferring the capability.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **A completed real project** — designed, built, and shipped to solve a real problem for a real user
2. **Version control with Git** — branching, commits, and collaborative development
3. **API integration** — connecting programs to external services and data sources
4. **Database fundamentals** — structured data storage and retrieval
5. **AI-assisted building** — using AI coding tools effectively with maintained understanding
6. **Teaching digital creation** — the ability to teach another person to build
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Building a Real Project

A real project has these characteristics:
- **It solves a real problem for a real user** (which can be yourself)
- **It works** — it does what it is supposed to do, reliably
- **It is finished** — shipped, deployed, or shared, not permanently "in progress"
- **It is maintained** — when something breaks or needs updating, you fix it

**Project ideas (choose one or propose your own):**
- A web application that tracks or manages something useful (a reading log, a habit tracker, a family recipe collection, a budget tool)
- A data analysis tool that processes a real dataset and produces useful insights
- A published portfolio website showcasing your work
- An automation system that handles a repetitive process for your household or community
- A functioning bot that performs a useful service
- A tool that integrates multiple services (pulls data from one source, processes it, delivers results through another)

**The process:**
1. **Define the problem.** What specifically does this project do? For whom? What does "done" look like?
2. **Design before coding.** Sketch the interface. Map the data flow. Identify the components. Planning first prevents rewriting later.
3. **Build incrementally.** Start with the smallest version that works (the "minimum viable product"), then add features. Ship early, improve continuously.
4. **Test as you build.** Does it work? Does it handle unexpected input? Does it fail gracefully when something goes wrong?
5. **Ship it.** Deploy it, share it, or put it in use. An unshipped project is a learning exercise, not a real project.

### Part 2: Version Control with Git

Git tracks every change you make to your code over time. This means you can see what changed, when, and why. You can go back to a previous version if something breaks. You can experiment on a branch without affecting the working version.

**Key concepts:**
- **Repository:** A folder tracked by Git. All changes are recorded.
- **Commit:** A snapshot of your code at a point in time, with a message describing what changed. Commit frequently — every time you complete a functional change.
- **Branch:** A parallel version of your code. You create a branch to work on a new feature without affecting the main code. When the feature works, you merge it back.
- **Push/Pull:** Sending your commits to a remote repository (GitHub/GitLab) and receiving others' commits.

**Why version control matters:** Without version control, you live in fear of breaking things. With it, you can experiment freely — if the experiment fails, you roll back. Version control is the safety net that makes bold building possible.

Start using Git from the first day of your real project. Commit regularly with descriptive messages. This is the digital equivalent of workshop discipline — clean workspace, labeled materials, documented process.

### Part 3: APIs — Connecting to the World

An **API (Application Programming Interface)** is a way for programs to talk to each other. Most modern digital services expose APIs that let your program request data, send messages, or trigger actions.

**Example:** The OpenWeatherMap API lets your program ask: "What is the current weather in this city?" The API responds with structured data — temperature, humidity, wind speed — that your program can use.

**What you can build with APIs:**
- A program that pulls today's weather and sends you a morning briefing
- A tool that fetches data from a service and formats it into a report
- An automation that monitors something and alerts you when conditions change
- A dashboard that aggregates data from multiple sources

**Working with APIs teaches you:** How the digital economy's plumbing works. Almost every modern service talks to other services through APIs. Understanding APIs is understanding how digital systems connect.

### Part 4: Database Fundamentals

A database is structured storage for information that can be efficiently queried, updated, and managed. Every web application, every online service, and every digital system that stores user data uses a database.

**Why databases matter:** When your project needs to store information that persists between sessions (user data, records, content), you need a database. A file works for simple cases. A database works for anything involving structured data, multiple records, or data that needs to be searched and filtered.

**Basic operations (CRUD):**
- **Create:** Add new records
- **Read:** Retrieve records matching criteria
- **Update:** Modify existing records
- **Delete:** Remove records

**SQL basics:** SQL (Structured Query Language) is the standard language for interacting with databases. Even a basic understanding — SELECT to read, INSERT to add, UPDATE to modify, WHERE to filter — opens enormous capability.

Start with SQLite (built into Python, no setup required). Build a simple database for your project. The goal is not database administration — it is understanding how every digital service stores and retrieves information.

### Part 5: AI Tools for Building

AI coding assistants can accelerate your building significantly — generating boilerplate code, explaining unfamiliar concepts, debugging errors, and translating between formats. Use them. But maintain your understanding.

**What AI does well in building:**
- Generating boilerplate and repetitive code
- Explaining error messages and suggesting fixes
- Translating between programming languages or formats
- Generating first drafts of functions you describe
- Writing documentation

**What AI does poorly in building:**
- Novel architecture decisions — it follows patterns from training data, not your specific needs
- Subtle bugs — AI-generated code can contain errors that are difficult to spot because the code looks plausible
- Domain-specific judgment — the AI does not know your project's requirements, constraints, or context
- Security — AI-generated code may contain vulnerabilities

**The rule:** Never include AI-generated code you do not understand. Every line that goes into your project should be code you can explain. If the AI generates something you do not understand, learn what it does before using it. AI accelerates capable builders. It does not replace the capability itself.

### Part 6: Teaching Digital Creation

The test of mastery: take someone who does not know how to build — a younger student, a peer, a family member — and teach them to create their first program, web page, or automation.

**The teaching process:**
1. Start with something achievable in one session (a "Hello World" program, a simple web page, a basic automation)
2. Explain concepts in the learner's language, not in technical jargon
3. Let them type — do not take over their keyboard
4. When they hit an error, guide them to the solution rather than fixing it yourself
5. Celebrate completion — they built something that works

The ability to transfer the capability is what distinguishes understanding from performance. A person who can build but cannot teach has skills. A person who can build and teach has mastery.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Completed Real Project
**Developing well:** The student has a working project that solves a real problem, uses version control, and has been shipped to at least one user. They can explain the architecture, the design decisions, and the trade-offs.

**Developing slowly:** The student has an incomplete project or a project that works in development but was never shipped.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Show me the project working. Who uses it? What feedback have you received?"

### 2. Professional Tool Proficiency
**Developing well:** The student uses Git naturally — committing regularly, branching for features, writing descriptive messages. They can work with at least one API and interact with a database.

**Developing slowly:** Git is used sporadically or not at all. APIs and databases remain theoretical rather than practiced.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Show me your commit history. What was your branching strategy? How does your project store data?"

### 3. AI-Assisted Building with Understanding
**Developing well:** The student uses AI tools to accelerate work but can explain every piece of code in their project. They can identify when AI-generated code contained errors and describe how they fixed them.

**Developing slowly:** The student relies on AI-generated code they do not understand. They cannot explain sections of their own project.

**Diagnostic questions:** Point to a specific function: "Explain what this does, line by line. Did you write this or did AI generate it? If AI, what did you change?"

### 4. Teaching Capability
**Developing well:** The student has successfully taught another person to build something functional. They can describe the teaching process, what the learner struggled with, and how they adapted.

**Developing slowly:** The student has not taught anyone or attempted to teach but could not adapt their explanation to the learner's level.
